observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous years’ recommendations and additions thereto and the LCE agreed to:
a. require Barangay officials to enhance/adopt the Municipal ordinances pertaining to the solid waste particularly on mandatory segregation of waste at source;
b. MENRO Office (i) strengthen implementation of existing policies on solid waste management to prevent commitment of prohibited acts enumerated in Section 48 of the RA No. 9003 and impose penalties to all violators, if necessary; (ii) adopt a safe closure and rehabilitation plan (SCRP) on the complete closure of the LGU’s open dumps instead of resorting to interim solution of embanking the land area and incorporate such strategies identified into the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan of the LGU; and (iii) submit a written explanation on the non-payment of the August to December 2023 billing of garbage collections.
c. Municipal Budget Officer to maintain the required registries to monitor the expenditures incurred on Solid Waste Management Program of the agency.
Additionally, we also recommended that the LCE allot budget or provide the required funding for the rehabilitation of the MRF and other infrastructure thereon and technical support in order to fully comply with the requirements of RA 9003 and achieve the eventual volume reduction of waste.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer will maintain the required registries to monitor the expenditures incurred on SWM Program of the agency, as instructed.

The Management through the MHRM will strengthen its screening process and conduct a review to comply and adhere to the audit recommendations. In addition, Municipal Accountant will ensure timely submission of requirements.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Local Chief Executive to direct/instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant/Supervising Officer to (i) strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts in order not to delay the verification and audit of transactions and the recording of transactions in the appropriate period; and (ii) observe the due dates in the submission to the Audit Team of the required financial reports and documents;
b. Officers and employees concerned to comply with the submission of adequate documents before forwarding any claim to the Accounting Unit for processing.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer to cause the submission of the above-mentioned lacking documents as required.
observations.managementAction: The Management will comply with the audit recommendation. In addition, the Municipal Accounting Office has completed the submission of CY 2023 vouchers and effected partial submission of the unsubmitted documents of CY 2024 last June 18, 2024.

1.15 We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive to
direct/instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the books of
account as to the discrepancies noted in the Cash in Bank accounts, including the
reversion of stale checks to appropriate accounts;
b. Municipal Treasurer, (i) as far as practicable, to take efforts that all issued
checks in payment of valid obligations are promptly released to the claimants at the
earliest possible time to prevent checks from becoming stale; and (ii) observe the
procedures on cancelling stale checks provided under Section 59 of the Manual on
the NGAS for LGUs. 3.12;
c. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to (i) reconcile and account
for the difference amounting to ₱4,809,821.78 between the Cashbooks and the
Accounting records of the Cash-in-Bank account and thereafter, record the necessary
adjustments; and (ii) conduct regular reconciliation of the accounts to establish the
correctness of both records; and
d. Further, we recommended that the LCE and the Local Sanggunian, thru the
recommendation of the Municipal Treasurer, immediately effect the proper closure
of the dormant bank account with an ending balance of ₱89,327.91 maintained at
Philippine National Bank – Angeles City branch.

2.7. We reiterated our recommendations and the Local Chief Executive (LCE)
agreed to:
a. require the concerned officials and employees/persons liable, in coordination
with the Municipal HRMO and Municipal Treasurer, to immediately settle all long-
overdue audit disallowances pursuant to COA Resolution No. 2015-031 dated August
20, 2015; and
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the balances of the subject
disallowances with pertinent records and thereafter, make the necessary adjustment
in the books.

3.10. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct/instruct the:
a. Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant, and Municipal Treasurer to
closely coordinate in monitoring the cash position of the LGU to ensure that all
disbursements are properly managed and controlled so that obligations are paid
accordingly; and
b. Municipal Accountant (i) to prepare and submit a SLs to facilitate
reconciliation and monitoring of the movement of each creditor’s account and thereby
come up with Schedule/Aging of Accounts Payable; and (ii) conduct a thorough review
and reconciliation of accounts.

4.22. We reiterated our previous recommendations and the LCE require/instruct
the:
a. the concerned department heads in (i) the reconstruction of property records
(e.g., Property Cards) and inventory-taking of all properties of the Municipality and,
henceforth, submit the Report of the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) and Report
on Physical Count of Local Road Network System, including the pertinent property
records in the prescribed format; and (ii) in coordination with the GSO Officer in
Charge, prepare and submit the IIRUP as a basis for recording the de-recognition
from the books of the unserviceable properties, including motor vehicles, and
immediately facilitate the disposal thereof to prevent further deterioration pursuant
to Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445; and
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) strengthen the controls in the handling and
recording procedures affecting PPE accounts; (ii) update/reconstruct PPELC,
lapsing schedule, and subsidiary records in order to facilitate reconciliation and draw
the necessary correcting entries for identified errors and omissions; (iii) improve
supervisory and review controls in the recognition of transactions and preparation of
financial statements to prevent the accumulation of errors and misstatements in the
financial statements; (iv) submit the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) for
the 13 units of motor vehicles; and (v) effect the necessary correcting entries in the
books, if any.

5.13. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the:
a. Municipal Treasurer, through its BPLO, to (i) continue/maintain its adaption
of the DICT web-based software on its registration system; and (ii) submit a copy of
Certified List of Real Property taxpayers to Municipal Accountant as basis for the
preparation of subsidiary ledger; and
b. Municipal Accountant to prepare a subsidiary ledger per individual account
and reconcile the accounts based on the Certified List of Real Property taxpayers so
that the necessary adjustment(s) in the books of accounts can be recorded to establish
the correct balances of the accounts.

6.21. We reiterated our recommendations and the Local Chief Executive (LCE)
agreed to instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer and the
BAC Secretariat, to: (i) submit the requisite documents noted, including a copy of the
project logbook as mandated; (ii) provide the “As-Built” plans for completed projects,
duly reviewed and certified by the Municipal Engineer’s Office; (iii) ensure that all
required information and documentation for infrastructure projects are complete
prior to the release of payment; and (iv) adhere to the provisions of the Revised
Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of R.A. No. 9184, Section 4(6) of P.D. No.
1445 as well as COA Circular No. 2012-001 concerning the documentary
requirements for infrastructure projects;
b. Municipal Accountant in coordination with the BAC Secretariat to submit the
approved DOLE CSHP as required, and comply with the provisions of DOLE
Department Order No. 13, Series of 1998 on CSHP; and
c. Municipal Accountant in coordination with the BAC Secretariat to submit the
documents or pictures showing the installation/posting of project
billboards/signboards, and comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004,
dated January 30, 2013 on posting of PPAs.

7.16. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to require the
GFPS to properly formulate GAD PPAs that are doable within the year, consider the
appropriate timing, and regularly monitor their implementation/utilization so that
necessary interventions may be introduced at the earliest time and amend the GPB, if
warranted, to achieve full implementation/utilization of GPB.

7.17. We also reiterated our previous recommendation and the LCE also agreed to
instruct the GFPS to adhere to the reportorial requirements prescribed in the JMC,
particularly in the monitoring and evaluation of the GAD mainstreaming activity, and
determine the judicious use of the GAD budget and the efficiency and effectiveness of
interventions in addressing gender issues.

8.14. We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the Local Chief
Executive require the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, in coordination
with the Municipal Engineer and BAC, to:
a. require end-users/offices or implementing units to formulate their respective
Project Procurement Management Plans (PPMPs) for their different programs,
activities, and projects (PAPs) for consolidations into the required APP, particularly
on DF PPAs; and subsequently, provide the Audit Team with a copy of the
approved/final APP;
b. along with the LDC, (i) improve the optimization of the 20 percent
Development Fund thru in-depth planning to ensure that only programs and projects
anchored on its medium and long-term development investment programs are well-
planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready and thereby achieve improved
quality of life for the marginalized Sector; and
8.15. We also recommended that the LCE, in coordination with the LDC, MPDO,
and Municipal Budget Office to present/express the PPAs in details charged under
the 20 percent DF in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) and Annual Budget as
required under Section 317, Item No. 3 of R.A No. 7160.
8.16. Management in their reply, commented the following:
The Management noted the findings and agreed with the recommendations. Further,
the Municipal Planning will review and improve the preparation of its APP/AIP.

9.22. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the (a)
LDRRM Officer in coordination with the CPD Officer to (i) include under a separate
caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of
previous years in the LDRRMIFP; and (ii) continue utilizing the fund for the
implementation of projects and activities for disaster preparedness, prevention and
mitigation, response, as well as rehabilitation and recovery; and (b) Municipal
Treasurer in coordination with the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the actual
transfer of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF to the Special Trust Fund under
the Trust Liability DRRM- account in accordance with the above-cited Circular.

10.12. We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive (LCE)
instruct the:
a. MENRO, in coordination with Barangay Officials, to strictly and regularly
monitor the segregation of mixed solid wastes at source; and further intensify the
information campaign drive particularly at the household level;
b. Municipal Budget Office to allot budget or provide the required funding for the
infrastructure/technical support of the Central MRF in order to fully comply with the
requirements of R.A. No. 9003 and, eventually, achieve a reduction in the volume
of wastes.

10.13. We also recommended that the MENRO, in coordination with Barangay
Officials, strictly enforce the implementation of existing environmental laws and the
imposition of penalties on all violators as provided in the Municipal Ecological Solid
Waste Management Ordinance No. 24, S. 2020.

11.8. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed, as Chairperson of the
LSB, to require the LSB, through the LSB Secretary in coordination with the
Municipal Accountant, to ensure the submission of SEF Utilization Reports
(Quarterly and Annually) to the concerned government agencies/offices; and monitor
the required timeline for the submission of SEF documents in accordance with Item
6.1 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.

12.11. We reiterated our recommendations and the Management agreed to require
the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer/Budget
Officer, and Project Coordinators in-charge of the fund transfers from NGAs, and
LGUs to:
a. submit to the Audit Team copies of the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) or
its equivalent document relative to the fund transfers received from NGAs and LGUs;
b. monitor submission to the concerned Source Agency (NGAS/LGUs) of
Liquidation Reports including the refund of unused balance from the funds received
by the Municipality; and
c. ensure submission of the documents related to fund releases/transfers to the
Municipality, and the corresponding liquidations thereof, and prepare and maintain
separate subsidiary ledger/record as required.

12.12. We also recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant,
Treasurer, and Budget Officer including Project Coordinators In-Charge to strictly
observe the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013, dated December 13, 1994, on the
releases/transfer of funds to the Municipal Government.

13.13. We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive, through
its Municipal Human Resource Management Officer to:
a. setup policy guidelines to address the issues on the necessity and propriety of
hiring job orders in order to limit the expenditures to programs/projects that are
highly essential to merit the recruitment of the same;
b. hire job order personnel based on necessity and skills intended for a specific
local project/s authorized by the Sanggunian for a period not exceeding six months;
c. avoid the hiring of job order personnel whose functions overlap/duplicate
those of the Municipality’s regular employees; and
d. coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to ensure the timely submission of
copies of the Approved Contracts and Accomplishment Reports for Job Orders as
requisite supporting documentation for the disbursement vouchers pertaining to the
payment for their services.

14.12. We reiterated our previous recommendations that the Local Chief Executive
to direct/instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant/Supervising Officer to (i) strictly comply with the
regulation on the rendition of accounts in order not to delay the verification and audit
of transactions and the recording of transactions in the appropriate period; and (ii)
observe the due dates in the submission to the Audit Team of the required financial
reports and documents;
b. Officers and employees concerned to comply with the submission of adequate
documents before forwarding any claim to the Accounting Unit for processing.

14.13. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the
Municipal Treasurer to cause the submission of the above-mentioned lacking
documents as required.

15.16. We reiterated our previous recommendations and the LCE agreed to instruct
the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and HRMO to:
a. review and analyze the discrepancies in the amount withheld and remitted as
shown in the above schedules and coordinate the result with the Government
Financial Institutions (GFIs) concerned for reconciliation of accounts; and thereafter,
make the necessary adjustments in the book of account; and
b. continuously observe the regulations and laws governing the withholding of
taxes, premiums, contributions, and amortizations, and accordingly, remit the same
intact within the prescribed period to the respective government agencies.

16.4. We recommended and the LCE agreed to continuously take necessary actions on
the settlement of the disallowances pertaining to prior years pursuant to the Rules and
Regulations on Settlement of Accounts (RRSA).

We commended the Management for ensuring that for CY 2023, the unexpended balance of the 5% LDRRMF was in compliance with the last paragraph of Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121, and Items 5.1.10 and 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
However, we reiterated our prior year’s recommendations and the LCE agreed to instruct the:
a. Municipal Budget Officer to prepare and maintain the prescribed Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations for LDRRMF pursuant to Item 5.1.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 based on the Obligation Requests of each expenditure; and
b. MDRRM Officer to (i) amend/supplement the Annual LDRRM Investment Plan particularly those PPAs defined in generic terms, and ensure that SB Appropriation Ordinance and/or resolutions are passed specifically on programming or utilization of the said appropriation; and (ii) refrain from charging ineligible expenditures classified as regular operating expenses to the LDRRMF and ensure that funds are used in accordance with Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121 and Item 4.3 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2014-1; and (iii) submit a justification on the (iii.a) propriety of charging the procurement of a 7-seater SUV-type motor vehicle worth ₱2,700,000.00 to the Fund and (iii.b) hiring of Certified Public Accountants for the preparation, upgrading and updating of the LDRRMF Plans.
observations.managementAction: A separate RAAO with prescribed format was prepared and submitted. The Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (DRRM) Plan of the Municipality of Magalang (2023-2025) has been duly reviewed by the Provincial Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office and the communication expenses charged to LDRRM fund were used as early warning system such as text blast and communication system for 27 barangays. This ensures proper coordination with the LGU during calamities and emergencies; The fuel expenses charged to the LDRRM fund were used for rescue operations.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer/Budget Officer, and Project Coordinators in-charge of the fund transfers from NGAs, and LGUs to:
a. submit to the Audit Team copies of the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) or its equivalent document relative to the fund transfers received from NGAs and LGUs;
b. monitor submission to the concerned Source Agency (NGAS/LGUs) of Liquidation Reports including the refund of unused balance from the funds received by the Municipality; and
c. ensure submission of the documents related to fund releases/transfers to the Municipality, and the corresponding liquidations thereof, and prepare and maintain separate subsidiary ledger/record as required.
We also recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant, Treasurer, and Budget Officer including Project Coordinators In-Charge to strictly observe the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013, dated December 13, 1994, on the releases/transfer of funds to the Municipal Government.

1.4 We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the Municipal
Treasurer to certify below the last entry on the cut off reports of Cashbook for
Cash in Treasury right after the cash examination as required in Section 26 of
MNGAS for LGUs.

2.7 We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer
(i) diligently review the CRAAF prior to distribution of report and submission
to the Audit Team to accurately account the Accountable Forms received,
issued or cancelled on a monthly basis; and (ii) cause the communication to the
concerned office upon detection of an erroneous reporting of Accountable
Forms issued and receipt by the agency.

3.11 We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the LCE instruct the
(a) Municipal Accountant to (i) continue the effort to reconcile the previous
years backlog in the preparation of MBRS; and (ii) make the necessary
adjustments in the books of account as to the discrepancies noted in the Cash
in Bank accounts including the reversion of stale checks to appropriate
accounts; and (b) Municipal Treasurer, (i) as far as practicable, to take efforts
that all issued checks in payment of valid obligations be promptly released to
the claimants the soonest possible time to prevent checks from becoming stale;
and (ii) cancel stale checks following the procedures provided for under Section
59 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs.

3.12 Further, we recommended that the LCE and the Local Sanggunian thru the
recommendation of the Municipal Treasurer to effect immediately the proper
closure of the dormant bank account with ending balance of ₱89,327.91
maintain at Philippine National Bank – Angeles City branch.

4.7 We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) require the concerned officials
and employees/persons liable in coordination with the Municipal HRMO and
Municipal Treasurer the immediate settlement of all long-overdue audit
disallowances pursuant to COA Resolution No. 2015-031 dated August 20,
2015; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the balances of
the subject disallowances with pertinent records and thereafter, make the
necessary adjustment in the books.

4.8 Management cited that the Accounting Unit will recognize the disallowances
totalling Php4,860,528.83 in the books under the Receivables-
Disallowances/Charges account for FY 2024.

5.40. We reiterated our recommendations and the LCE agreed to:
a. instruct the concerned department heads to review the requirements
and follow COA Circular No. 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of PPEs and
COA Circular No. 2015-008 in order to have a verifiable data on the existence,
condition and accountability of the Municipality’s PPEs;
b. reconstitute an Inventory Committee with competent and dedicated
members, to include at least one member each from the Accounting and
Property Department of the LGU pursuant to Item 5.2. of COA Circular 2020-
006 dated January 31, 2020 in order to address the recurring issues in the
establishment of the accuracy of the PPE balances particularly on the (i)
reconstruction of property records (e.g. Property Cards) and inventory-taking
of all properties of the Municipality and henceforth, submit the Report of the
Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) and Report on Physical Count of Local
Road Network System including the pertinent property records in the
prescribed format; and (ii) in coordination with GSO Officer in Charge, to
prepare and submit the IIRUP as basis in recording the de-
recognition from the books of the unserviceable properties including motor
vehicles, and immediately facilitate the disposal thereof to prevent further
deterioration pursuant to Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445; and (iii) together with
the Municipal Appraisal Committee to declare all identified real properties duly
owned by the Municipality and come up with report/recommendation of the
fair and reasonable valuation based on pertinent records; and
c. Municipal Accountant to (i) strengthen the controls in the handling and
recording procedures affecting PPE accounts; (ii) update/reconstruct PPELC,
lapsing schedule and subsidiary records in order to facilitate reconciliation and
draw the necessary correcting entries for identified errors and omissions; (iii)
48
improve supervisory and review controls in the recognition of transactions and
preparation of financial statements to prevent the accumulation of errors and
misstatements in the financial statements; and (iv) effect the necessary
correcting entries in the books, if any.

6.12. We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the:
a. Municipal Appraisal Committee headed by the Municipal Assessor
and/or the Municipal Engineer to submit (i) the lot plan/location map duly
prepared and signed by a geodetic engineer and certified copies of approved
subdivision plans from the Bureau of Lands, DENR – R3 to pinpoint the exact
location and determine the metes and bounds of the purchased lot; and (ii) the
Committee’s report/recommendation on the establishment of the fair and
reasonable appraisal of the market value of the said property;
b. Municipal Treasurer/Municipal Assessor to (i) cause the proper titling
of the purchased lot in the name of the Municipality and subsequently submit
the pertinent documents as enumerated above to establish regularity of real
estate transaction; and (ii) provide explanation on why the Municipality bears
the relevant transfer taxes on the transfer of said property in consonance with
Section 173 of RA No. 8424; and
c. Municipal Accountant to submit all the above-mentioned documentary
requirements including the certified true copy of the Transfer Certificate of
Title to the Audit Team to establish the propriety and regularity of the payment.

6.13. During the exit conference, the Management commented that the requisite
documents for the purchase of the aforementioned lot are all complete. These
include the location plan/map prepared and signed by the geodetic engineer,
certified copies of approved subdivision plans from the Bureau of Lands, and the
certified true copy of the Transfer Certificate of Title which will be submitted to the
Audit Team the soonest.

6.14. Furthermore, the Municipal Assessor, in his reply, commented that under Section
199 of NIRC, the transaction made by the LGU is not listed on the exempted from
the payment of Documentary Stamp Tax. In addition, as per Section 196 of the
National Revenue Code that documentary stamp tax is only exempted on donor’s
tax; and as per Revenue Regulation No. 9-2000, the documentary stamp taxes under
Title VII of the NIRC is a tax imposed against “the person making, signing, issuing,
accepting, or transferring” the document or facility evidencing the aforesaid
transactions. Thus, in general, it may be imposed on the transaction itself or upon
the document underlying such act. Any of the parties thereto shall be liable for the
full amount of the tax due: Provided, however, that as between themselves, the said
52
parties may agree on who shall be liable or on how they may share on the cost of
the tax.

6.15. As a rejoinder, the absence of stipulation on the Deed of Absolute Sale on who
would shoulder the expenses related its execution ang registration, the vendor
should bear the cost of the sale’s execution and registration in adherence with
Article 1487 of the New Civil Code.

6.16. Furthermore, Section 481 of the Local Government Code prohibits the LGU from
engaging the services of a private lawyer. Further, a Decision dated on 23 February
2015 was rendered by the Sandiganbayan in Crim Case No. SB-08-CRM-0018, has
established, albeit impliedly, that when a city has no legal officer, it behooves upon
the provincial legal officer to extend legal assistance to an notarize for free the
Deed of Sale executed by the City, whenever there is no available city legal officer.

6.17. Thereby, the Audit Team further recommended that the following incidental
charges incurred by the LGU from the execution and registration of the afore-cited
subject lot be lawfully refunded to the LGU, to wit:
