7.24. We recommended that the Management/HOPE to secure first an Authority to
Purchase Motor Vehicles in accordance with Department of Budget and
Management (DBM) Budget Circular (BC) Nos. 2022-1 and 2022-1A dated
February 11, 2022 and March 1, 2023, respectively, and COA Circular No.
2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, and submit justification/explanation why
Authority to Purchase Motor Vehicles from the DILG was not sought on the
acquisition of one unit motor vehicle/pick-up.

7.25. We also recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the BAC Secretariat, to (i)
submit the requisite documents; and (ii) henceforth, ensure that the required
documents particularly for procurement for goods are complete before
payment;
b. Acting General Services Officer to (i) prepare and submit the
Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) as required by Volume II,
NGAS Manual for Local Government Units; and (ii) furnish the COA Audit
Team with copies of all Purchase Orders (POs) and Perfected Contracts
including supporting documents and the Notice of Delivery as required and
strictly observe the regulations prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-001
dated February 12, 2009 on Contracts/Purchase Orders; and COA Circular
No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996 on Notice of Delivery; and
c. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to strictly comply with the
provisions of RA 9184 and its 2016 Revised IRR particularly on (i) Sections
21.2 and 21.2.1 on advertising and posting of the Invitation to Bid/Request for
58
Expression of Interest; (ii) Sections 32 and 32.2.1, on Bid Evaluation; (iii)
Sections 34 and 34.3 on Post Qualification; (iv) Section 37.1.6 on the posting of
Notice of Award; and (v) Sections 37.4.2 on the posting of the Approved
Contract and Notice to Proceed.

7.26. In their reply, the Management commented the following:
❖ The Management did not secure an Authority to Purchase Motor Vehicles
since the vehicles purchased were medical ambulances, rescue vehicles and
garbage truck. As per DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2022-105 on the
Revised Policy Guidelines on Motor Vehicles purchase/acquisition, use, and
rental by the Local Government Unit, under Section 5.5 The Local Chief
Executive is the approving authority for the acquisition of motor vehicles
such as under Section 5.5.2.1 Specific-purpose vehicles e.g. medical
ambulances, waste management or environmental sanitation equipment,
vehicles for mass transport when necessary, in the interest of public service;
while, the pick-up/rescue vehicle acquired by the agency will primarily be
used by the MDRRM Council for monitoring during calamities.
Additionally, this vehicle will be used for rescue operations and immediate
transport of victims during emergencies.
❖ The Supervising Officer of the Accounting Unit in coordination with the
Acting GSO has already prepared Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment
(ARE) for the medical ambulances and vehicles purchased.

7.27. As a rejoinder, the Management must still comply with the preparation of Authority
to Purchase Motor Vehicle (APMV) in accordance with Items 6.0 and 601 of DBM
Circular Nos. 2022-1 and 2022-1A dated February 11, 2022 and March 1, 2023,
respectively. This requirement was later adopted by DILG Memorandum Circular
No. 2022-105 dated August 23, 2022. The APMV issued by the appropriate
approving authority, in this case, the LCE, will serve as basis for the agency in
acquiring the motor vehicle.

8.8. We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer, through its BPLO, agreed to
(a) levy and collect taxes and fees in accordance with the Municipality’s Local
Revenue Code; (b) require the submission of sworn statement of gross
receipts/sales or certified copies of income tax return in order to come up with
a more reliable basis for computation of business taxes; (c) in addition, to
regularly conduct examination of the books of accounts and confirm the
declared gross receipts with other government agencies in the renewal of
business licenses of taxpayers to verify the accuracy of the declared gross
receipts; and (d) establish a stratified schedule to set a certain income level
standard for various business entities after careful considerations of various
industry factors as basis for presumptive income level to be embodied in the
agency’s local tax ordinance.

8.9. Further, we recommended and the Management likewise agreed to coordinate
with the DICT Technical Team for the conduct of appropriate training and
capability building to ensure that configurations made in the system conform
with the rates established in the agency’s Local Ordinance and to administer a
compensatory control to prevent miscalculation on fees and detect errors in
generation of audit reports.

9.30. We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations and the LCE agreed to
require the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, in coordination with
the Municipal Engineer and BAC, to:
a. require end-user/offices or implementing units to formulate their
respective Project Procurement Management Plans (PPMPs) for their
different programs, activities, and projects (PAPs) for consolidations into the
required APP and subject to review and updating at least every six months or
as often as needed or required by the LCE; and
b. along with the LDC, (i) improve the optimization of the 20 percent
Development Fund thru in-depth planning to ensure that only programs and
projects anchored on its medium and long-term development investment
programs are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready and
thereby, achieve improved quality of life for the marginalized sector; and (ii)
any amendments thereof proposed to the LDC will be subject to be approval
by the Sanggunian Bayan such as those mentioned above;

9.31. We also recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Municipal Budget
Officer to (i) prepare the OBr as accurately and completely indicate the PPAs
with their OBr number, OBr date, responsibility center, function/program/project
and other references; (ii) maintain a Registry of Appropriations, Allotment and
Obligations for Current Year Appropriations and Continuing Appropriations as
required by Section 2.2 of COA Circular No. 2006-002 dated January 31, 2016
and thereafter, (iii) prepare a SAAOB for 20 percent DF continuing
appropriations.

10.24. However, we reiterated our prior year’s recommendations and the LCE agreed
to instruct the:
a. Municipal Budget Officer to prepare and maintain the prescribed
Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations format for LDRRMF
pursuant to Item 5.1.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12,
2012 based on the Obligation Requests of each expenditure; and
b. MDRRM Officer to (i) amend/supplement the Annual LDRRM
Investment Plan particularly those PPAs defined in generic terms, and ensure
that SB Appropriation Ordinance and/or resolutions are passed specifically on
programming or utilization of the said appropriation; (ii) refrain from
charging ineligible expenditures classified as regular operating expenses to the
LDRRMF and ensure that funds are used in accordance with Section 21 of R.A.
No. 10121 and Item 4.3 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2014-1; and (iii)
submit a justification on the (iii.a) propriety of charging the procurement of a
7-seater SUV-type motor vehicle worth ₱2,700,000.00 to the Fund and (iii.b)
hiring of Certified Public Accountants for the preparation, upgrading and
updating of the LDRRMF Plans.

11.9 We reiterated our previous years’ recommendations and additions thereto and
the LCE agreed to:
a. require Barangay officials to enhance/adopt the Municipal ordinances
pertaining to the solid waste particularly on mandatory segregation of waste at
source;
b. MENRO Office (i) strengthen implementation of existing policies on
solid waste management to prevent commitment of prohibited acts enumerated
in Section 48 of the RA No. 9003 and impose penalties to all violators, if
necessary; (ii) adopt a safe closure and rehabilitation plan (SCRP) on the
complete closure of the LGU’s open dumps instead of resorting to interim
solution of embanking the land area and incorporate such strategies identified
77
into the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan of the LGU; and (iii) submit a
written explanation on the non-payment of the August to December 2023
billing of garbage collections.
c. Municipal Budget Officer to maintain the required registries to monitor
the expenditures incurred on Solid Waste Management program of the agency.

11.10 Additionally, we also recommended that the LCE allot budget or provide the
required funding for the rehabilitation of the MRF and other infrastructure
thereon and technical support in order to fully comply with the requirements
of RA 9003 and achieve the eventual volume reduction of waste.

12.16 We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the GFPS to properly
formulate GAD PPAs that is doable within the year, consider the appropriate
timing and regularly monitor their implementation/utilization so that
necessary interventions may be introduced at the earliest time and amend the
GPB, if warranted, to achieve full implementation/utilization of GPB.

12.17 We also reiterated our previous recommendation and the LCE agreed to
instruct the GFPS to adhere to the reportorial requirements prescribed in the
JMC, particularly in the monitoring and evaluation of the GAD
mainstreaming activity and determine the judicious use of the GAD budget
and the efficiency and effectiveness of interventions in addressing gender
issues.

13.11. We recommended that the LCE require the Acting GSO or Municipal
Treasurer to handle the monitoring of fuel consumption of the Municipality
including the issuances of DTT to the concerned municipal officials without
prejudice to those who claim transportation allowance.

13.12. Also, we reiterated our prior year’s recommendations and the LCE agreed to
instruct the (a) Municipal Budget Officer to maintain the required registries to
prevent incurrence of expenditures in excess of allotments or budget
overrun/overdraft; (b) Acting GSO/Municipal Treasurer to enjoin all
respective department heads to (i) regulate the issuance of serially numbered
Driver’s Trip Tickets duly supported with Purchase Order (PO) to ensure that
claims are duly authorized; and (ii) prepare the Monthly Report of Official
Travels and the Report of Fuel Consumption and submit the same to the
Auditor for verification to ensure that expenditures for fuel consumption are
reasonable, effectively controlled and properly accounted for and thus,
minimize expenses on fuel; and (c) Municipal Accountant to submit copy of the
notarized contract with FCC General Merchandise Corp. and Finizzio Fuel
Station as required under Item 3.1.1 of COA Circular 2009-001 dated February
12, 2009.

14.25. We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Municipal Engineer,
and BAC Secretariat to (i) submit the “As-Built” plans of completed projects,
reviewed and certified by the Municipal Engineer’s Office; (ii) submit the
noted requisite documents, including the copy of project logbooks as
required; (iii) ensure that the required information and documents for
infrastructure projects are complete before payment; and (iv) comply with
the provisions of the 2016 RIRR of R.A No. 9184, COA Circular No. 2012-
001 dated June 14, 2012, Section 4 (6) of P.D. 1445, and DPWH Department
Order No. 216 dated November 16, 2000 on the documentary requirements
for infrastructure projects;
b. Municipal Engineer to require the contractor to install/post the
signboard/billboard containing relevant information about the project at all
sites where the PPA/s will be implemented in accordance with the full
disclosure policy of the government;
c. Municipal Accountant in coordination with the BAC Secretariat to
submit the documents or pictures showing the installation/posting of project
billboard/signboard, and also, the proof/evidence on the use of Personal
Protective Equipment (PPE)/DOLE approved CSHP as required, and comply
with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 on posting of PPAs, and
DOLE Department Order No. 13, Series of 1998 on CSHP; and
d. HOPE, through the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), to strictly
comply with Sections 37.2.2, and 37.4.1 of the 2016 RIRR of R.A No. 9184 on
the timely preparation and issuances of the Contract and Notice to Proceed
(NTP), respectively, to avoid delays in the project implementation and
completion.

15.9 We reiterated our recommendations and LCE agreed to direct the:
a. Municipal Budget Officer to provide enough funding for the insurance
of all insurable properties pursuant to the provisions of R.A. No. 656, or
Property Insurance Law, to protect the interest of the government and be
indemnified in case of damage or loss due to unforeseen event;
b. General Services Officer (GSO)/Municipal Treasurer to abide with the
requirements set forth in RA No. 656, also known as Property Insurance Law,
to insure all insurable properties of the Municipality with the Property
Insurance Fund of the GSIS; and
c. Municipal Accountant to update/reconstruct PPELC primarily of
insurable properties to facilitate the determination of the value of properties
subject of insurance.

15.10 Further, we recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Chairman of the
Appraisal Committee to cause the appraisal of the insurable properties and
other assets owned by the Municipality and thereby come up with a report on
Property Inventory Form as required under COA Circular No. 2008-002.

16.14. We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant
and Supervising Officer of the Accounting Unit to (a) constantly coordinate
with the MSWD Officer on the submission of the requisite documents related
to fund transfer to the Municipality; (b) prepare and maintain separate
subsidiary ledger/record as required by Section 4.5 of COA Circular No. 94-
013 dated December 13, 1994; and (c) monitor the submission of the
corresponding Liquidation Report including the refund of unused balance, if
any, to the Source Agency.

17.19. We reiterated our recommendations and the LCE agreed through its
Municipal Human Resource Management Officer to (a) prepare and issue
policy guidelines to address the issues on the necessity and propriety of hiring
job orders in order to limit the expenditures to programs/projects that are
highly essential to merit the recruitment of the same; (b) hire job order
personnel based on necessity and skills/competencies and who should be
identified/attributed to specific local projects authorized by the Sanggunian for
a period not exceeding six months; (c) comply with submission of Daily Time
Records and Accomplishment Reports with specific output to evaluate the
necessity and validity of all JO personnel employed; and (d) rationalize the
daily rates of job-order personnel based on qualifications and skillset.

17.20. Further, we recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the:
a. Municipal Budget Officer to regularly update the RAAO to facilitate
the preparation of the monthly list of signed and issued Obligation Requests
with complete information such as ObR control number, name of payee,
particulars/nature of transaction, amount, Function/Program/ Project (FPP),
complete budget name, name of responsibility center, date signed and account
code, and furnish the same to the Audit Team Leader for audit and monitoring
purposes;
b. Municipal Accountant and its Supervising Officer to ensure that
disbursement vouchers for payment of JO wages are supported with monthly
Daily Time Records and Accomplishment Report with specific output certified
correct by the assigned authority/head;
c. Municipal Treasurer ensure that claims for payment are duly signed by
the JO personnel and/or authorization letter in case the claimant is other than
the JO.

18.8. We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and the LCE agreed to
instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to support
and coordinate with each other in the preparation of the complete RAAOs and
SAAOB in compliance with COA Circular 2006-002 and COA Accounting
Circular Letter No. 2007-002, respectively, to facilitate monitoring of
expenditures and balances of appropriations in order to prevent incurrence of
expenditures in excess of allotments.

19.11 We reiterated our recommendations and the LCE agreed (a) require the
Municipal Accountant and Supervising Officer of the Accounting Unit to (i)
strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts in order not to
delay the verification and audit of transactions and the recording of
transactions in the appropriate period; and (ii) observe the due dates in the
submission to the Audit Team of the required financial reports and documents;
and (b) enjoin all officers and offices concerned to comply with the submission
of adequate documents before forwarding any claim to the Accounting Unit
for processing.

20.19 However, we reiterated our previous recommendations and the LCE agreed to
instruct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and HRMO to (a)
review and analyze the discrepancies in the amount withheld and remitted as
shown in the above schedules and coordinate the result with the Government
Financial Institutions (GFIs) concerned for reconciliation of accounts; and (b)
cause the immediate remittance to Pag-IBIG Fund of the unremitted loan
repayment amortizations and henceforth, ensure consistent deduction of
premiums and loan amortizations and prompt remittances thereof.

21.4 We recommended and the LCE agreed to, through the Municipal Accountant,
require all the persons liable to immediately refund the full amount of
disallowance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the 

Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer and BAC Secretariat, to (i) submit to the Audit Team the certified copy of project logbook, DOLE approved CSHP and the lacking documentary requirements; and (ii) comply with the provisions of the Revised IRR of R.A No. 9184 on the documentary requirements particularly for infrastructure projects; and 

 Municipal Engineer to ensure that the contractor erect signboard with prescribed size and information at the project location as soon as the award has been made in accordance with Item 2.2.3 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 and take pictures of the signboard with the project location as background during construction for audit and documentary purposes.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) and other concerned
Municipal officials meticulously and judiciously plan actions to be taken during
occurrence of calamity and delegate the acceptance and inspection of purchases
including the receipt of stock items upon issuance thereof to the concerned municipal
officials in consonance with Sections 118 and 121 of the MNGAS for LGUs and
inconsistent with the vested power, duties and functions under Section 444 of RA No.
7160 or the Local Government Code 1991 .
Also, we reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the (a) BAC
require every offices/end-user units to prepare PPMP then consolidate the same for
the preparation of the Annual Procurement Plan which incorporate the procurement
projects undertaken thru negotiated procurement modality during a State of Public
Health Emergency; (b) Municipal Budget Officer maintain the required registries to
prevent incurrence of expenditures in excess of allotments or budget overrun; (c)
Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Department Heads in-charge of
procurement, (i) ensure that all POs and other notice of deliveries are adequately
prepared and all necessary information pertaining to purchases especially the
technical description and specifications are supplied in the POs and Sales Invoice, and
furnish the Audit Team whether printed copy or online/electronic copy of PO within
five working days upon issuance thereof; and (ii) utilize a sound warehouse
management system that will streamline the receipt and distribution processes of
inventoriable stocks and prepare and maintain the supplies ledger cards for all
inventories based on the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) and
prepare the necessary entry to record the expenditures using appropriate expenditure
accounts; (d) Municipal Accountant ensure the adequate documentation of
transactions particularly those welfare goods intended for distribution to
beneficiaries and food expenses consumed during vaccination caravan; and (e)
General Services Officer or Municipal Treasurer accept first the deliveries of items
purchased by the local government units and the authorized inspector/s conduct
inspection of the delivered items for conformity with specification in the purchase
order.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the

Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to strictly monitor and enforce compliance with the rendition of accounts and submission of financial reports and documents within the prescribed period/due dates for the timely audit of transactions and communications of reportable deficiencies; and

concerned officers/employees to comply with the submission of complete supporting documents before forwarding any claim to the Accounting Office for processing.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to support and coordinate with each other in the preparation of the complete RAAOs in compliance with COA Circular 2006-002 and the SAAOB pursuant to COA Accounting Circular Letter No. 2007-002 to be used in the preparation of the SCBAA.
observations.reason: (Reiterated in Part II of this Report)

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive meticulously and judiciously plan actions to be taken during occurrence of calamity and delegate the awarding of contract under Negotiated Procurement (Emergency Cases) to any official of the procuring entity (PE) except to the BAC Chairperson or members pursuant to Section 11.2.5 of the 2016 revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We also recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the

BAC to require every offices/end-user units to prepare PPMP for the preparation of the Annual Procurement Plan which incorporate the procurement projects undertaken thru negotiated procurement modality during a State of Public Health Emergency; 

Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Department Heads in-charge of procurement, to (i) ensure that all POs and other notice of deliveries are adequately prepared and all necessary information pertaining to purchases especially the brand name/specifications are supplied in the POs and Sales Invoice, and furnish the Audit Team whether printed copy or online/electronic copy of PO within five (5) working days upon issuance thereof; and 

(ii) utilize a sound warehouse management system that will streamline the receipt, packaging and distribution processes of relief packages and install an internal control mechanism to safeguard the relief goods from possible theft, loss and wastage; and 

Municipal Accountant to (i) ensure the adequate documentation of transactions particularly the intended beneficiaries or the receiving barangay (end-users) of the packages; and 

(ii) abide with the tax compliance requirements set forth in BIR RR No. 17-2003 dated March 31, 2003 to impose tax accordingly on all transactions of the Municipality to local/resident supplier of goods.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the 

Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to liquidate the SAP-1 and return the unutilized funds to the DWSD; 

 Municipal Accountant and Municipal Social Welfare & Development (MSWD) Officer to submit the SAC Forms and photo of the beneficiaries during the actual distribution of SAP-1; and  

MSWD Officer to ensure that the (i) prescribed procedures on validation of eligible beneficiaries were strictly observed throughout the implementation of the SAP or financial subsidies before the actual payout to pave the way for a final clean Certified List of SAP beneficiaries; and (ii) investigate and submit a written explanation for the unmatched names/SAP Code between that of the Municipality, payrolls and the DSWD’s List of SAP-1 beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer record the collections and deposits daily or as transaction occurs and strictly adhere to the regulation that collections be deposited intact with Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB) preferably on the following banking day.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly monitor the updating process of accounting records particularly Subsidiary Ledgers and General Ledger for Cash – Local Treasury Account and to strictly comply with the above regulations in order to have an updated Subsidiary Ledger Balances.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive instruct the 

General Services Officer or the Municipal Treasurer, together with the Inventory Committee (IC), to (i) expedite the reconstruction of PPE records particularly the Property Cards (PC) and diligently carry-out their duties and functions, (ii) prepare and submit the comprehensive RPCPPE duly reconciled with the accounting and property records; and (iii) set the appraised value of unserviceable/obsolete properties to pave the way for the preparation of the IIRUP; 

Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Assessor to come up with the fair value of lands owned by the Municipality that have not yet been recognized in the books owing to lack of basis for valuation and exert efforts in completing the necessary documents to facilitate the transfer of ownership; and 

 Municipal Accountant to (i) strengthen the controls in the handling and recording procedures affecting PPE accounts; (ii) update/reconstruct PPELC, lapsing schedule and subsidiary records in order to facilitate reconciliation and draw the necessary correcting entries for identified errors and omissions; and (iii) improve supervisory and review controls in the recognition of transactions and preparation of financial statements to prevent the accumulation of errors and misstatements in the financial statements and effect the necessary correcting entries in the books, if any.

We reiterated our recommendations and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive instruct the BAC to (i) subject all procurements to public bidding unless the conditions are met for the use of shopping as alternative mode of procurement; and (ii) comply strictly with Section 54.1 of the IRR of RA No. 9184 on the prohibition of splitting of contract; and direct all offices concerned and the BAC Secretariat to prepare a comprehensive Annual Procurement Plan containing all the requirements of the Municipality for the ensuing year especially those goods/items for distribution to barangay.

observations.reason: (Reiterated in Part II of this Report)

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the 

GFPS to (i) submit the annual GAD AR in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01; and 

(ii) submit the said reports to the Audit Team within the prescribed period pursuant to COA Circular No. 2014-001; and 

 Municipal Budget Officer to (i) henceforth, allocate at least 5% of the total budget for the accomplishment of GAD PPAs; and 

(ii) prepare and maintain the RAAO for GAD plan and budget as basis for preparation of the monitoring and evaluation reports for GAD PPAs.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the MENRO and the Focal Person for Solid Waste Management in coordination with Barangay Officials to strictly and regularly monitor the segregation of mixed solid wastes at source; and further intensify the information campaign drive particularly at the household and business level with focus on Solid Waste Management (SWM) and ordinances issued by the Municipality; and enforce the implementation of existing environmental laws and the imposition of penalties to all violators as provided in the Municipal Ecological Solid Waste Management Ordinance No. 3 and Segregation at Source Ordinance No. 5.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive direct the 

Municipal HRMO to comply with the Labor Code of the Philippines on the compulsory coverage of all the LGU’s manpower complement with government-mandated deductions of PhilHealth, Pag-IBIG, and SSS contributions; and 

 Municipal Accountant (i) to ensure consistent deduction of premiums and prompt remittances thereof; and 

(ii) cause the reconciliation of Pag-IBIG prior to closing of books in order to address the issue on the abnormal account balance.
observations.reason: (Reiterated in Part II of this Report)

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations and the Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the

Municipal Accountant to (i) address the issue on the doubtful accuracy of the Due to BIR account balance and submit to the Audit Team the pertinent journal entry vouchers on the said account; and (ii) henceforth, strictly comply with BIR Revenue Regulations No. 2-98 relative to the withholding of taxes from income payments to suppliers and honorarium of consultants; and 

Municipal HRMO to abide with the tax compliance requirements set forth in BIR RMC No. 58-2018 to require all Job Order personnel to register with the BIR and submit Income Payee’s Sworn Declaration of gross receipts before the next payroll.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive, through the Municipal Accountant, 

direct all persons responsible to submit the required documents to settle the notice of suspension; and 

require all the persons liable to immediately refund the full amount of disallowance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the 

(a) BAC and its Secretariat to (i) subject all procurement to public bidding unless the conditions are present for the use of small value procurement or other alternative mode of procurement; (ii) prepare a complete and comprehensive Annual Procurement Plan containing all the requirements of the Municipality within a year using the format prescribed by Section 3.2 of GPPB Circular No. 01-2009 dated January 20, 2009 and pursuant to the Procurement Manual for Local Government Units; 


(b) Municipal Health Office together with other health unit officials, to maintain (i) Stocks Inventory Report which shows the quantity of medicines received, the complete description of medicines received, the quantity of medicines utilized within a month, and balance of medicines in quantity per item at the end of the month; (ii) Monthly Summary of Medicines Utilized which shows the quantity of medicines dispensed to patients taken from Utilization Report and complete description of medicines; (iii) Annual Inventory Report which shows the quantity of medicines remaining in stocks as of year-end and complete description of per items of medicines; (iv) install an inventory monitoring mechanism to monitor the expiration of medicines; and 

(c) Acting Municipal Accountant to record all purchases of drugs and medicines in the books using the Inventory account and use the Monthly Summary of Medicines Utilized prepared by RHUs as basis in recording of utilized medicines in Drugs and Medicines Expense account.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the 

(a) Municipal Budget Officer to submit a written explanation on the specific source of budget of the payroll of consultants with amount of ₱3,829,500.00 together with job order personnel with amount of ₱21,902,845.28; 

(b) instruct the Acting Human Resource Management Officer to (i) submit a copy of notarized contract as required under Item 3.1.1 of COA Circular 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009; (ii) abide with the conditions and procedures provided by existing laws, rules and regulations in hiring consultants to minimize expenditures and maximize utilization of available manpower resource; and (iii) engage only experts in their respective fields; and 

(c) direct the acting Municipal Accountant to (i) check adequacy of the documentary requirements, such as monthly accomplishment reports, to establish regularity of claims; and (ii) strengthen the controls in the handling and recording procedures of accounting transactions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the 

(a) Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to investigate the reason for the scaling of the constructed road considering that it was newly constructed only five months ago and demand from the contractor the repair thereof at no additional cost to the government; and 

(b) Acting Municipal Accountant, along with other concerned officials to (i) check the adequacy of the documentary requirements to establish regularity of claims by referring to COA Circular No. 2012-001 and the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and other relevant memoranda; and (ii) cause the return of the unutilized balance of the fund transfer to the source agency.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the 

(a) Members of the BAC and Technical Working group to (i) submit a written explanation on the items specifications/ quantity of delivered equipment different from the Approved Budget of Contract, Purchase Request and Invitation to Bid; (ii) henceforth, thoroughly review the bid documents as to accuracy and completeness; and 

(b) Acting Municipal Accountant, along with other concerned officials to check the adequacy of the documentary requirements to establish regularity of claims by referring to COA Circular No. 2012-001 and the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and other relevant memoranda.

observations.managementAction: The Management has assisted the barangay officials in putting up their own MRF by providing materials and scouting for suitable MRF area.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to comply with the required appropriation of one percent (1%) of IRA for Local Council for Protection of Children; and separate it from the GAD budget to optimize the utilization of both funds.

We recommended that the members of the Municipal School Board 

(a) improve compliance to the guidelines under Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 dated January 19, 2017 of the DepEd, DBM and DILG particularly on the allocation and utilization of the SEF for priority items as listed in Item 4 of the JC; and 

(b) henceforth, submit all the program of works to the Municipal Engineering for his approval and for him to prepare corresponding detailed engineering plans as required under Paragraph 3(i) of Annex “A” of Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (RIRR) of RA No. 9184.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant in coordination with other offices concerned, work on the completion of the documentary deficiencies for submission to the Audit Team; and henceforth, check the adequacy of supporting documents to establish the propriety and regularity of the claims.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the 

(a) Human Resource Management Officer to hire job order personnel based on necessity and skills/ competencies and who should be identified/attributed to specific local projects authorized by the Sanggunian for a period not exceeding six months in order to limit the expenditures on programs that are highly essential to merit the recruitment of these personnel; and 

(b) Municipal Accountant to support disbursements of funds for services rendered by job order personnel with accomplishment reports to establish validity and propriety of payment.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the Acting Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the (i) signed certified correct/reviewed subsidiary ledgers and general ledgers of all FS accounts; and (ii) signed certified correct/reviewed cash/check disbursement journals, cash receipts journals and general journals complete with particulars and summary of journal entries of each monthly journals tallied with postings at subsidiary/general ledgers and working trial balance.
We also recommended that the LCE instruct the Acting Municipal Accountant to (i) reconcile the identified variances between accounting journals and FS on 16 accounts; and (ii) henceforth, always review accounting journals to ensure that all transactions are recorded in their appropriate accounts.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations and would reflect all the necessary adjustments in the beginning balance of CY 2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
record collections and deposits daily or as transaction occurs and strictly adhere to the regulations that collections be deposited intact with Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB) preferably on the following banking day, and in exceptional cases, within five (5) days after receipt;
ensure the correctness and reliability of entries in the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF); and
adjust the noted variance in the Cashbook of Cash-in-Vault account.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.  In Addition, the Management mentioned that they have already adjusted the noted variance in their respective cashbooks and would maintain updated subsidiary ledgers.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive extend, as far as practicable, the necessary logistical support to enable the Accounting Office to work on the backlog on the preparation of bank reconciliation statements and instruct the Municipal Accountant to (a) study on how to fully utilize the capabilities of existing accounting staff  in order to address the reporting deficiencies of the Accounting Office; (b) prepare an action plan with time frame to address the backlog in the preparation and submission of BRS; and (c) make the necessary adjustments in the books of account as to collections not found in the Cashbook for Cash-in-Vault and Cash Receipts Journal.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to (a) immediately require accountable officers to settle the outstanding cash advances and submit the corresponding liquidation reports together with the duly numbered and accomplished vouchers/payrolls and supporting documents, otherwise impose the necessary sanctions as provided for in the circular; (b) ensure that cash advances are settled and reported on within the prescribed period of liquidation through compliance with the reporting and monitoring requirements in the Report of Disbursements and maintenance of Subsidiary Ledgers; and (c) refrain from granting additional cash advances unless the former are fully liquidated/settled.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive direct the Acting Municipal Accountant to prepare a subsidiary ledger per individual account based on certified list of real property taxpayers and henceforth, regularly reconcile the balance of RPT/SET Receivable accounts with the list of taxpayers with paid/unpaid real property tax dues maintained by the Municipal Treasurer then draw journal entry vouchers to adjust the balances thereof.
The Management mentioned that they are currently updating the subsidiary ledgers per real property/special education tax account.  They also mentioned that any necessary adjustments approved by the Provincial Treasurer, Provincial Assessor and the Sanggunian Panlalawigan would be reflected accordingly.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the:
Municipal Engineer to submit the lot plan/location map duly prepared and signed by a geodetic engineer and certified copies of approved subdivision plans from the Bureau of Lands, DENR – R3 to pinpoint the exact location and determine the metes and bounds of the purchased lots;
Municipal Treasurer to cause the proper titling of the purchased lots in the name of the Municipality and subsequently, submit the pertinent documents as enumerated above to establish the regularity of real estate transaction; and
Acting Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Appraisal Committee, cause the submission of the in-house appraisal report to ascertain the fair market value of the land and the other pertinent documents as enumerated above to establish the propriety and validity of claims.
During the Exit Conference, the Management submitted most of the lacking documents except for the blue print copy of the Lot Plan duly signed by a Geodetic Engineer, copy of Extrajudicial Settlement of Estate with Absolute Sale and Waiver of Rights registered with the Registry of Deeds and Certificate of No Improvements.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive provide the legal basis for the payment of honorarium to the Municipal Engineer acting as OIC-Municipal Budget Officer, otherwise require the refund of the unauthorized honoraria granted thereof.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) concerned personnel to abide strictly with regulations on the use of motor vehicles and refrain from utilizing government funds for gasoline consumption of privately-owned vehicles to prevent disallowances and regulate the issuance of serially numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets duly supported with Purchase Order (PO) to ensure that claims are duly authorized; (b) Municipal Accountant to submit copy of the notarized contract with FCC General Merchandise Corp. and Triple V Gasoline as required under Item 3.1.1 of COA Circular 2009-001, the supporting sales invoice together with the corresponding DTT and PO to substantiate the billing with discrepancies, otherwise cause the refund of the overpayment; and (c) concerned drivers and officials to prepare Monthly Report of Official Travels and the Report of Fuel Consumption and submit the same to the Auditor for verification to ensure that expenditures for fuel consumption are reasonable, effectively controlled and properly accounted for.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.  However, they would submit documents to prove that the vehicles were not privately owned but government properties.  Further, those vehicles only have conduction stickers because their plate numbers were not yet released by the Land Transportation Office (LTO), hence, they were mistakenly identified as privately owned.

We reiterated our recommendations and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
Municipal Health Office, together with other health unit officials, to maintain (i) Stocks Inventory Report which shows the quantity of medicines received, the complete description of medicines received, the quantity of medicines utilized within a month, and balance of medicines in quantity per item at the end of the month; (ii) Monthly Summary of Medicines Utilized which shows the quantity of medicines dispensed to patients taken from the Utilization Report and complete description of medicines; (iii) Annual Inventory Report which shows the quantity of medicines remaining in stocks as of year-end, complete description of items of medicines; (iv) an inventory monitoring mechanism to monitor the expiration of medicines; and
Acting Municipal Accountant to record all purchases of drugs and medicines in the books using the Inventory account and use the Monthly Summary of Medicines Utilized prepared by RHUs as basis in the recording of utilized medicines under the Drugs and Medicines Expense account.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and other officials who reviewed the disbursements (a) submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents to establish the regularity of the claims of the mentioned treasury staff and all those who availed of the PCL-LA Training Program as basis for the next audit action; (b) require the immediate refund of excess travel expenses of ₱8,800.00 from the said treasury staff, and (c) abide strictly with CSC regulations and other pertinent rules and regulations in the administration of scholarship in the Municipality so that each eligible employee has equitable opportunity to enjoy such personnel benefit, if funds are available.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.  In addition, the Management informed the Audit Team that the concerned personnel had already paid the excess travel expenses of ₱8,800.00 as evidenced by OR No. 1770257 dated 12/28/2018.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) BAC to (i) subject all procurements to public bidding unless the conditions are met for the use of shopping as alternative mode of procurement; and (ii) comply strictly with Section 54.1 of the IRR of RA No. 9184 on the prohibition of splitting of contract; and (b) all offices concerned and the BAC Secretariat to prepare a comprehensive Annual Procurement Plan containing all the requirements of the Municipality for the ensuing year.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.  They reasoned that they resorted to shopping as mode of procurement to immediately accommodate the needs and requests of the barangays.  Further, they were contemplating of giving cash donations to barangays instead of goods or materials in order to forestall exceeding the threshold for competitive bidding.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Agriculturist and BAC Secretariat, to (i) submit the mentioned lacking documents; (ii) submit the List of Recipients (farmer-beneficiaries) of different barangays and strictly comply with Section 4 (6) of PD 1445 on complete documentation; and (iii) ensure that the required documents particularly for procurement of goods are complete before payment; and (b) Bids and Awards Committee to strictly comply with the provisions of the revised IRR of R.A No. 9184 particularly (i) Sections 21.2 and 21.2.1 on advertising and posting of the Invitation to Bid/Request for Expression of Interest; (ii) Sections 32 and 32.2.1, Rule IX on Bid Evaluation, (iii) Sections 34 and 34.3, Rule X on Post-Qualification; (iv) Section 37.1.6 on the posting of Notice of Award; and (v) Section 37.4.2 on the posting of the Approved Contract and Notice to Proceed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the (a) Municipal Accountant to (i) address the issue on the doubtful accuracy of the Due to BIR account balance and submit to the Audit Team the pertinent journal entry vouchers on the said account; and (ii) henceforth, strictly comply with BIR Revenue Regulations No. 2-98 relative to the witholding of taxes from income payments; and (b) Municipal HRMO to abide with the tax compliance requirements set forth in BIR RMC No. 58-2018 to require all Job Order personnel to register with the BIR and submit Income Payee’s Sworn Declaration of gross receipts before the next payroll.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.  Further, they explained that the ₱190,073.85 adjustment was to correct the erroneous amount of journal entry for check no. 379821 dated August 29, 2018.

We commended the Management for its effort to comply with the imposition of mandatory deductions for GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth contributions, and remitting the same within the prescribed period.
However, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the (a) Municipal Treasurer to remit accordingly the contributions within the prescribed period as mandated by PhilHealth Circular No. 001 S.2014 to prevent the imposition of undue interests and surcharge; (b) Municipal HRMO to comply with RA No. 10606 on the compulsory coverage of all employees of mandatory government deductions of PhilHealth contributions; (c) Municipal Accountant to conduct a reconciliation of accounts of the Due to GSIS and PhilHealth and effect the necessary adjustment in the books if warranted; and (d) responsible official for the late remittances to refund the amount of interests and surcharges imposed.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.  They further mentioned that some personnel not withheld with PhilHealth contributions were already paying contributions as self-employed or covered under the PhilHealth ng Masa sponsored program; thus, the Management would provide evidences on such.  Nevertheless, they would require the concerned personnel to file PhilHealth Member Registration Form to change their status from self-employed to employed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the General Services Officer (GSO) to abide with the requirements set forth in RA No. 656, also known as Property Insurance Law, to insure all insurable properties of the Municipality with the Property Insurance Fund of the GSIS.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.  Also, they mentioned that they would request the Sanggunian Bayan to set aside budget for the insurance of the properties.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive (a) address with urgency the manpower and competency issues faced by the Municipal Accounting Office; (b) require the Acting Municipal Accountant to (i) strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts in order not to delay the verification and audit of transactions and the recording of transactions in the appropriate period; and (ii) observe the due dates in the submission to the Audit Team of the required financial reports and documents; and (c) enjoin all officers and offices concerned to comply with the submission of adequate documents before forwarding any claim to the Accounting Office for processing.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Acting Municipal Accountant, in coordination with other Offices concerned, (a) work on the completion of the documentary deficiencies; and (b) ensure that the required documents for each type of transaction are completely attached to the DV’s before their processing and payment.
We also recommended and Management agreed that the BAC Secretariat strictly comply with (i) Sections 17 and 17.1 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 on the preparation of the Bidding Documents (ii) Sections 21.2 and 21.2.1 thereof on the posting of the Invitation to Bid/Request for Expression of Interest in the PhilGEPS website, and (iii) Paragraph 3(i) of Annex “A” thereof on submission of the Program of Works including PERT/CPM network.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) Human Resource Management Officer to hire job order personnel including barangay liaison officers based on necessity and skills/competencies and identified/attributed to specific local projects authorized by the Sanggunian for a period not exceeding six months in order to limit the expenditures on programs that are highly essential to merit the recruitment of these personnel and avoid exceeding the 45% Personal Services Limitation; (b) Acting Municipal Accountant to (i) support disbursements of funds for services rendered by job order personnel with accomplishment reports to establish validity and propriety of payment; (ii) record the payroll of all job order and casual personnel under Salaries and Wages - Casual/Contractual account instead of Other General Services or Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses accounts; and (iii) record the payroll for magna carta benefits under Hazard Pay account instead of Other General Services account for fair presentation of the financial statements; (c) HRMO to require all job order personnel, including barangay liaison officers, to prepare and submit monthly accomplishment reports pursuant to Paragraph 4.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (d) Acting Municipal Budget Officer to (a) submit a written explanation on (i) charging the payroll of job order and casual personnel to the budget for Provision for Personnel Development Program (PPDP) and Integrated Rural Development Program (IRDP) without prior approval from the Sanggunian Bayan considering that these budgets have vague or broad description on how to spend the funds; and (ii) the ₱11,803,269.99 budget overrun of PPDP; and (b) limit the budget allocation for the salaries of job order to avoid exceeding the 45% Personal Services Limitation.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.  However, they would seek legal advice on the possibility of job order personnel’s exclusion in the computation of 45% PS limitation.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) Municipal Engineer and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to ensure strict compliance with Sections 6.1 and 6.2 of Annex “E” of the IRR of R.A No. 9184 on the withholding of the ten percent (10%) retention on progress payments to contractor to cover possible uncorrected discovered defects and third-party liabilities; (b) Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer and BAC Secretariat, to submit the Project Logbook including Inspection Report by the Agency’s Authorized Engineer, Certificate of Completion and Certificate of Acceptance on the above-mentioned infrastructure projects in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 and DPWH Department Order No. 216; (c) Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer and BAC Secretariat, to (i) submit the lacking documents/documentary requirements communicated thru AOM; (ii) ensure that the required documents particularly for infrastructure projects are complete before payment; (iii) comply with the provisions of the IRR of R.A No. 9184, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and DPWH Department Order No. 216 on the documentary requirements, particularly for infrastructure projects; and (d) Bids and Awards Committee to strictly comply with the provisions of the IRR of R.A No. 9184, particularly on Sections 21.2 and 21.2.1 on advertising and posting of the Invitation to Bid/Request for Expression of Interest; Section 37.1.6 on the posting of Notice of Award;  Sections 37.4.1 and 37.4.2 on the issuance of Notice to Proceed and posting of Approved Contract and Notice to Proceed; and Section 37.2.3 (f) requiring that Construction Safety and Health Program (CSHP) be part of the contract.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.  However, they would verify if the contractor submitted surety bond in lieu of 10% retention.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a)  LDC and other concerned department to formulate 20% DF programs, projects and activities which are aligned and in accordance with the City’s priority programs, projects and activities as embodied on its LDIP/CDP and monitor and execute accordingly based on the timeline provided thereto; (b) Municipal Budget Officer to (i) accomplish the RAAO for the 20% DF and for Continuing Appropriations of previous years; and (ii) refrain from charging ineligible projects, programs and activities not specifically found in the 20% DF budget; and (c) Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, to (i) comply with the reportorial and monitoring requirements as prescribed in DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2008-156 on the submission of Annual/End-of-Term Accomplishment Report for all AIP and LDIP programs/projects; (ii) render a comprehensive Quarterly Report of the Publicized Government Projects/Programs/Activities on Goods and Infrastructures in compliance with COA Circular 2013-004; and (iii) along with the LDC, improve the optimization of the 20% Development Fund thru judicious planning and compliance with the guidelines on the utilization of the DF under DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1.
The Management would submit a copy of duly signed/approved supplemental budget to show that they have appropriated enough amount for the 20% Development Fund.  Also, the Management would provide a copy of the LDIP approved by the Sanggunian Bayan and make necessary amendments on the CDP to reflect the current needs of the Municipality for approval of Sanggunian Bayan.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive, thru the OIC-MENRO and Environmental Care Management Unit Head (a) involve closely the Barangay Officials and Civic Leaders in the implementation of the existing environmental laws and intensify the information campaign at the household and business level on the ordinances issued by the Municipality; (b) formulate solid waste management programs that would encourage and motivate the public to give their cooperation and full support; and (c) enforce the penalties provided for in the Municipal Ecological Solid Waste Management Ordinance No. 3 and Segregation at Source Ordinance No. 5 to ensure compliance thereof.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) Acting Municipal Budget Officer to fully accomplish all entries in the Obligation Requests (ObR) and regularly update the RAAO to facilitate the preparation of the monthly list of signed and issued Obligation Requests with information such as ObR control number, name of payee, particulars/nature of transaction, amount, Function/Program/ Project (FPP), complete budget name, name of responsibility center, date signed and account code, for submission to the Audit Team Leader for audit and monitoring purposes; (b) Acting Municipal Accountant to ensure that disbursement vouchers for processing and payment are supported with duly accomplished Obligation Request such as FPP per approved appropriation, account code, and signature of requestor on the certification of charges to appropriation/allotment necessary, lawful and under direct supervision portion of ObR especially payroll transactions; (c) Municipal Treasurer to ensure that claims for payment are duly supported with the necessary documents, including the duly accomplished ObR and the signature of the requestor; and (d) Acting Municipal Budget Officer and Acting Municipal Accountant to support and coordinate with each other in the preparation of the complete Registries of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) and Statement of Appropriations, Allotments, Obligations and Balances (SAAOB) for CY 2018 to be used in the presentation of Statement of Comparative Budget and Actual Amount in the CY 2018 Financial Statements, and every accounting period thereafter.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive institute appropriate administrative sanctions against the erring official/s concerned for habitual neglect of duty if no significant improvement is evident in the financial and budgetary reporting of the Municipality after a reasonable period.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.  They also mentioned that they were continually looking for a qualified Municipal Budget Officer to focus and address the pressing issues in the budgeting.

We recommended that the
Local Chief Executive to extend, as far as practicable, the necessary logistical support to enable the Accounting Office to work on the backlog of the preparation of bank reconciliation statements; and

Municipal Accountant to prepare an action plan with time frame to address the backlog in the preparation and submission of BRS.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Assessor obtained necessary documents from the Municipal Treasurer and had initial consultation with the Land Registration Authority to obtain basis for recording and reconciliation.
A memorandum dated July 23, 2018 was issued by the Municipal Mayor regulating the fuel consumption of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive 

(a) address with urgency the manpower and competency issues faced by the Accounting Office;  

(b) instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to provide budget for the trainings of accounting staff; and 

(c) require the Acting Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the (i) signed certified correct/reviewed subsidiary ledgers and general ledgers of all FS accounts, (ii) signed certified correct/reviewed monthly working trial balance for the Audit Team to review on how they arrived at the account balances of CY 2017 Financial Statements based on the submitted accounting journals; and (iii) signed certified correct/reviewed cash/check disbursement journals, cash receipts journals and general journals complete with particulars and summary of journal entries of each monthly journals tallied with postings at subsidiary/general ledgers and working trial balance. (printed copies and soft copies)
observations.managementAction: A meeting has been conducted with Acting Municipal Accountant, HRMO and Municipal Mayor to address the staffing pattern of accounting office.
MDRRMO coordinated with OCD for training schedules. ICS has been approved by the Municipal Mayor.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the 

(a) GAD Focal Person/ Municipal Budget Officer, in coordination with concerned department heads, to prepare CY 2017 GAD Accomplishment Report based on the approved GAD Plan and Budget and treasury/accounting records; and 


(b) Municipal Planning and Development Officer (CPDO) to install a computerized system for the establishment of the GAD database or Community Based Monitoring System (CBMS), which could provide the data requirements for the GAD Database, in time for the next GAD Plan and Budget preparation.
observations.managementAction: The Management conducted an initial planning for the computerized database/ CBMS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant continue to withhold and remit the GSIS membership contributions and loan repayments within the prescribed period.

(a) to verify with HDMF the details of the reported unremitted loan amortizations for resolution of the issue;

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the (a) BAC and its Secretariat to (i) subject all procurement to public bidding unless the conditions are present for the use of small value procurement or other alternative mode of procurement; (ii) prepare a complete and comprehensive Annual Procurement Plan containing all the requirements of the Municipality within a year using the format prescribed by Section 3.2 of GPPB Circular No. 01-2009 dated January 20, 2009 and pursuant to the Procurement Manual for Local Government Units; (b) Municipal Health Office together with other health unit officials, to maintain (i) Stocks Inventory Report which shows the quantity of medicines received, the complete description of medicines received, the quantity of medicines utilized within a month, and balance of medicines in quantity per item at the end of the month; (ii) Monthly Summary of Medicines Utilized which shows the quantity of medicines dispensed to patients taken from Utilization Report and complete description of medicines; (iii) Annual Inventory Report which shows the quantity of medicines remaining in stocks as of year-end and complete description of per items of medicines; (iv) install an inventory monitoring mechanism to monitor the expiration of medicines; and (c) Acting Municipal Accountant to record all purchases of drugs and medicines in the books using the Inventory account and use the Monthly Summary of Medicines Utilized prepared by RHUs as basis in recording of utilized medicines in Drugs and Medicines Expense account.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) Municipal Budget Officer to submit a written explanation on the specific source of budget of the payroll of consultants with amount of ₱3,829,500.00 together with job order personnel with amount of ₱21,902,845.28; (b) instruct the Acting Human Resource Management Officer to (i) submit a copy of notarized contract as required under Item 3.1.1 of COA Circular 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009; (ii) abide with the conditions and procedures provided by existing laws, rules and regulations in hiring consultants to minimize expenditures and maximize utilization of available manpower resource; and (iii) engage only experts in their respective fields; and (c) direct the acting Municipal Accountant to (i) check adequacy of the documentary requirements, such as monthly accomplishment reports, to establish regularity of claims; and (ii) strengthen the controls in the handling and recording procedures of accounting transactions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive  (a) instruct the Municipal Assessor to hasten the identification of duplicate records and tax-exempted real property units for adjustment in the Integrated Taxation Management System; (b) direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a subsidiary ledger per individual accounts based on the “cleansed” certified list of real property taxpayers as a result of the aforementioned efforts; and (c) henceforth, regularly reconcile the balance of RPT/SET Receivable account with the list of taxpayers with paid/unpaid real property tax dues maintained by the Municipal Treasurer then draw journal entry vouchers to adjust the balances thereof.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the (a) Acting Human Resource Management Officer to hire job order personnel based on necessity and skills/competencies and who should be identified/attributed to specific local projects authorized by the Sanggunian for a period not exceeding six months in order to limit the expenditures on programs that are highly essential to merit the recruitment of these personnel and avoid exceeding the 45% Personal Services Limitation; (b) Municipal Accountant to support disbursements of funds for services rendered by job order personnel with accomplishment reports to establish validity and propriety of payment and record the payroll of job order and casual personnel in Salaries and Wages - Casual/Contractual account instead of Other General Services account for fair presentation of financial statements; (c) Acting HRMO to require the job order personnel to prepare and submit monthly accomplishment reports pursuant to Paragraph 4.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (d) Municipal Budget Officer to a) submit a written explanation (i) on charging the payroll of job order and casual personnel to the budget for Provision for Personnel Development Program and Integrated Rural Development Projects and Services without prior approval from Sanggunian Bayan considering that these budgets have vague or broad description on how to spend the funds, (ii) on how and where the ₱10.8 million excess payroll of job order and casual personnel was taken from the Municipal Budget; and b) to increase the Personal Services budget to accommodate the payroll of job order and casual personnel in the next budget preparation but not to exceed the 45% Personal Services Limitation.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Accountant (i) comply with the submission of the documentary requirements, as enumerated above (ii) ensure that the required documentary requirements supporting of all purchases especially medicines are present; and (iii) record all purchases of drugs and medicines in the books using the Inventory account and use the Monthly Summary of Medicines Utilized prepared by MHO as basis in recording of utilized medicines in Drugs and Medicines Expense account; (b) BAC Secretariat  submit screenshots of posted notices at PhilGEPS website in CY 2016 and submit written explanations if the invitation to bid for the procurement of medicines purchased from Ladyjen Medical Trading and RDW Trading was not posted at PhilGEPS website; (c) Municipal Health Officer (MHO) and BAC Secretariat (i) invariably provide the complete description of medicines in Purchase Request especially the unit of doses in milligram (mg) or volume/weight of bottle or ointment in millilitre (ml); and (ii) purchase medicines only within the Philippine National Drug Formulary (PNDF); and (d) Municipal Health Officer  maintain (i) Stocks Inventory Report which shows the quantity of medicines received from the Municipality, the complete description of medicines received, the quantity of medicines utilized within a month, and balance of medicines in quantity per item at the end of the month; (ii) Utilization Report which shows the complete name of patient, date of visit, complete address, age, gender, heath complaints of patient, diagnosis, complete description and quantity of dispensed medicines; (iii) Monthly Summary of Medicines Utilized which shows the quantity of medicines dispensed to patients taken from Utilization Report and complete description of medicines; and (iv) Annual Inventory Report which shows the quantity of medicines remained in stocks as of year-end and complete description of per items of medicines.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal officials and employees concerned to (a) abide strictly with the COA regulations on the use of motor vehicles and refrain from utilizing government funds for gasoline consumption of privately owned vehicles; (b) indicate the plate number and the specific purpose of travel in the trip tickets; (c) exert effort to save funds under capital outlay and appropriate for the acquisition of brand new service vehicles in the succeeding budget year; and (d) require the refund of all gasoline reimbursements amounting to ₱219,066.64 made by officials receiving fixed transportation allowance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) Municipal Engineer and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to ensure strict compliance with (i) Sections 6.1 and 6.2 of Annex “E” of  the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 requiring 10% retention on payments to contractor to cover for possible uncorrected discovered defects and third party liabilities; (ii) Sections 37.2.1 and 39.1 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 requiring the contractor to post performance security upon contract signing to guarantee his faithful performance of his obligations under the contract; (iii) DOLE DO No. 13, Series of 1998 on the submission of construction safety and health program duly approved by DOLE; and (iv) DPWH Department Order No. 216 on the submission of copy of Diary or Log and photographic records with imprinted dates; (b) Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer and BAC to submit the lacking documents as required by COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 12, 2012 and Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and (c) Municipal Engineer to conduct thorough review on the computation of ABC to ensure its compliance with the prescribed rules and regulations specifically on Department Order No. 22, series 2015 dated February 18, 2015 before the same is submitted to the Office of the Municipal Mayor for approval.

We recommended that the (a) Local Chief Executive (i) prioritize the placement of a full-time MDRRM Officer to focus on core functions of the MDRRMO and the three mandatory staff personnel at the MDRRMO to handle the administration and training, research and planning, and operations and warning to effectively and efficiently address the requirements of the above-cited existing rules and regulations on disaster preparedness, mitigation, recovery and response; (b) MPDO fast track the preparation of the Municipality’s Comprehensive Development Plan (CDP), Comprehensive Land-Use Plan (CLUP) and Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Plan (MDRRMP) in order that disaster risk management activities could be integrated to the development plan of the Municipality to prevent further loss of lives and properties; and    (c) Municipal Budget Officer maintain a separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations for LDRRMF pursuant to Item 5.1.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 so that the Audit Team could verify the accuracy of the LDRRMF utilization report.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant, in coordination with BAC Secretariat, to (i) ensure that claims are supported with all the necessary documents and properly signed by authorized officials before any payment is made; and (ii) submit the required documents stated above for the evaluation of the Audit Team and subsequent audit action (b) Municipal Agriculturist to present the two units of Four- Wheel Drive Mini Tractor for ocular inspection of the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and ECMUH (a) address urgently the issues surrounding the non-completion of the Municipality’s 10-year solid waste management plan to pave the way for its completion and subsequent endorsement for approval to the National Solid Waste Management Commission; (b) involve closely the Barangay officials and civic leaders in the strict implementation on the existing environmental laws and intensify the information campaign at the household and business level on the ordinances issued by the Municipality; (c) seek the help of private companies/organizations in the conduct of a more aggressive and intensive information campaign on the proper segregation of waste; and (d) formulate solid waste management programs that would encourage and motivate the public to give their cooperation and full support.

We recommended that the Members of the Provincial Local School Board (a) prepare a balanced budget with conscious and concerted efforts of observing compliance with the legal and regulatory requirements on SEF budgeting, allocation and utilization such as Department of Education (DepEd), Department of Budget and Management (DBM) and Department of Interior and Local Government (DILG) Joint Circular No. 1, s. 1998 dated April 14, 1998 (amended by DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017)  and refrain from accommodating expenditures for activities for which the DepEd has the direct mandate to execute; and (b) request for inputs from the Deped official/School Division Superintendent in the prioritization of SEF projects and expenditures in accordance with the DepEd criteria and with due regard to the provisions of laws, rules and regulations prescribing the limitations on the use of the fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the GAD Focal Point/Person, who is also the Municipal Budget Officer, to (a) henceforth provide 5% of the total budget for the accomplishment of Gender and Development (GAD) programs/projects/ activities (PPAs); and (b) evaluate the causes of the non-implementation of the three GAD PPAs to gain lessons or inputs thereon.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Planning and Development Officer (CPDO) to install a computerized system for the establishment of the GAD database in time for the next GAD Plan and Budget preparation.

We recommended that the (a) Acting Municipal Accountant  (i) strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts and financial reports in order not to delay the verification and audit of transactions; and (ii) observe the due dates in the submission to the Audit Team of the required financial reports and documents; (iii) submit the 108 disbursement vouchers amounting to ₱28,814,951.19 to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension;  (b) top management (i) augment the manpower complement of the Accounting Department through, as far as practicable, reassignment from other departments of personnel with relevant competencies; (ii) enjoin all officers and offices concerned to comply with the submission of adequate documents before forwarding any claim to the Accounting Department for processing.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) in coordination with other offices involve, work on the completion of the documentary deficiencies; and (b) henceforth, ensure that the required documents for each type of transaction are submitted/attached to the DVs before they are processed and paid.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) Municipal Budget Officer to fully accomplish all entries in the Obligation Requests (ObR) and regularly update the RAAO to facilitate the preparation of monthly list of signed and issued Obligation Requests with information such as ObR control number, name of payee, particulars/nature of transaction, amount, Function/Program/ Project (FPP), complete budget name, name of responsibility center, date signed and account code, for submission to the Audit Team Leader for audit and monitoring purposes; (b) Acting Municipal Accountant to ensure that disbursement vouchers for processing and payment are supported with duly accomplished Obligation Request such as FPP per approved appropriation, account code, control number assigned to the ObR, responsibility center, and signature of Municipal Budget Officer (MBO) on the certification of existence of available appropriation portion of ObR; (c) OIC-Municipal Treasurer to  ensure that claims for payment are duly supported with the necessary documents, including the duly accomplished ObR and the signature of the Municipal Budget Officer to establish their regularity; and (d) Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to support and coordinate with each other in the preparation of the complete Registries of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) and Statement of Appropriations, Allotments, Obligations and Balances (SAAOB) for CY 2016 to be used in the presentation of Statement of Comparative Budget and Actual Amount in the CY 2016 Financial Statements, and every accounting period thereafter.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive institute appropriate administrative sanctions against the erring official/s concerned for habitual neglect of duty if no significant improvement is evident in the financial and budgetary reporting of the Municipality after a reasonable period.

We commended the officials and employees concerned for being compliant with the GSIS regulations and recommended that they continue being compliant in the deduction and remittance of the correct amounts within the prescribed period.
We commended the Municipality for its consistency in complying with the regulation on the withholding of the appropriate taxes and the remittance thereof within the prescribed period.
We recommended that Local Chief Executive, through the Accountant, require all the persons liable on the disallowance to immediately refund the full amount of disallowance.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer maintain deposits only with authorized government depository banks or with banks with authority/approval from DOF and Monetary Board of BSP pursuant to DOF Department Circular No. 001-2015.
observations.managementAction: Transferred all funds from Porac Bank to Land Bank and discontinued depositing collections to Porac Bank.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive 

(a) cause the refund of excessive amount of CNA Incentive paid to employees totaling ₱262,708.33 and the amounts paid to consultants totaling ₱302,501.00 for being contrary to DBM BC No. 2015-2;

(a) Municipal Budget Officer, the OIC-Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant  coordinate with one another to  

(i) establish the  amount of unutilized balance of the LDRRMF based on the realized income from regular sources or compute for the unutilized balance backed up by cash for transfer to the special trust fund;

(ii) effect the transfer of the amount established under the first procedure from the general fund to a special trust fund; and

(i) provide 5% of the total budget from regular sources for the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund pursuant to Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121; and

(c) Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer (MDRRMO) formulate a Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan every year to serve as guide in the utilization of LDRRMF and submit it to the LCE for approval.
